name: LaTeX
summary: The gold standard for academic typesetting. Unmatched math rendering and citation management. Steep learning curve but ubiquitous in STEM publishing. TeX Live distribution runs natively on HPC.

name: Typst
summary: Modern alternative to LaTeX with fast compilation, readable markup, and real-time collaboration via typst.app. Written in Rust, single binary. Growing academic adoption but not yet accepted by most journals.

name: Groff
summary: GNU troff — the traditional Unix document formatting system. Powers man pages. Extremely lightweight and available on virtually every Unix system. Niche use outside manpages today.

name: ConTeXt
summary: TeX-based typesetting system with integrated design philosophy. More consistent interface than LaTeX but smaller community. Excellent for complex page layouts and multilingual typography.

name: SILE
summary: Modern typesetter inspired by TeX but built with Rust and Lua. Uses XML or SILE-native input. Excellent multilingual and OpenType support. Small but active community.

name: Pandoc
summary: The universal document converter. Converts between dozens of formats with Lua filter extensibility. Foundation for Quarto and R Markdown. Essential infrastructure for any document toolchain.

name: Mammoth
summary: Converts .docx to clean semantic HTML. Focuses on meaning over formatting — maps Word styles to HTML elements. Available in JS, Python, and Java. Good for CMS ingestion pipelines.

name: WeasyPrint
summary: Python HTML/CSS to PDF renderer. Uses CSS Paged Media for print layouts. No browser dependency — pure Python with Cairo backend. Excellent for generating PDFs from HTML templates.

name: Prince
summary: Commercial HTML-to-PDF converter with best-in-class CSS Paged Media support. Used by publishers for production-quality PDF output. Free for non-commercial use (with watermark).

name: wkhtmltopdf
summary: HTML to PDF converter using Qt WebKit. Archived and unmaintained since 2023. Still widely used in legacy pipelines. Recommend migrating to WeasyPrint or Playwright PDF export.

name: CommonMark
summary: Standard specification for Markdown with reference implementations in multiple languages. Resolves ambiguities in original Markdown. Foundation for GitHub Flavored Markdown and many tools.

name: MDX
summary: Markdown with JSX components. Enables interactive React components inside documentation. Powers Docusaurus content. Tight coupling to React ecosystem is both strength and limitation.

name: Markdoc
summary: Stripe-developed Markdown superset with custom tags and validation. Renders to a serializable AST, framework-agnostic output. Simpler mental model than MDX — no arbitrary JS execution in content.

name: unified
summary: AST-based content processing ecosystem. Remark (markdown), rehype (HTML), and retext (natural language) share a unified plugin pipeline. Foundation for MDX, Docusaurus, Astro content processing.

name: markdown-it
summary: Fast CommonMark-compliant Markdown parser with plugin API. Used by VitePress and many Node.js projects. Extensible via syntax plugins for tables, footnotes, task lists, etc.

name: Goldmark
summary: CommonMark-compliant Markdown parser for Go. Default parser for Hugo. Fast, extensible with AST transformers. GFM extensions available. Pure Go, no CGo dependencies.

name: Quarto
summary: Next-gen scientific publishing system from Posit. Multi-language (Python/R/Julia), multi-output (HTML/PDF/DOCX/RevealJS). Built on Pandoc. Rapidly becoming the standard for reproducible research documents.

name: R Markdown
summary: Pioneer of literate programming in data science. R-first but supports Python via reticulate. Predecessor to Quarto — still widely used in R community. Posit recommends Quarto for new projects.

name: Jupyter Book
summary: Build publication-quality books from Jupyter notebooks and MyST Markdown. Built on Sphinx. Excellent for computational narratives, course materials, and research monographs.

name: Pweave
summary: Scientific report generator for Python inspired by Sweave (R). Weaves code and results into LaTeX, Markdown, or RST. Lightweight but largely superseded by Quarto and Jupyter Book.

name: nbdev
summary: Develop Python libraries entirely in Jupyter notebooks. Exports clean .py modules, auto-generates docs via Quarto, runs tests from notebook cells. Opinionated but productive for library authors.

name: Org-mode
summary: Emacs-based literate programming, task management, and document authoring system. Babel supports 50+ languages for code execution. Extremely powerful but tightly coupled to Emacs ecosystem.

name: Codebraid
summary: Pandoc filter that executes code blocks in Markdown and inserts output. Supports multiple languages via Jupyter kernels or direct execution. Lightweight alternative to Quarto for simple literate docs.

name: Reveal.js
summary: HTML presentation framework with nested slides, Markdown content, and plugin ecosystem. Default backend for Quarto presentations. Speaker notes, PDF export, and extensive theming.

name: Slidev
summary: Developer-focused presentation tool using Markdown and Vue components. Built on Vite for instant HMR. Inline Monaco editor, recording, and PDF/SPA export. Best for technical talks with live coding.

name: Marp
summary: Markdown-to-slide converter with VS Code integration. CSS-themed slides from plain Markdown. Exports to HTML, PDF, and PPTX. Simpler than Reveal.js — good for quick, clean presentations.

name: Beamer
summary: LaTeX document class for presentations. Academic standard for conference talks — integrates natively with LaTeX math, citations, and TikZ diagrams. Steeper learning curve than HTML-based alternatives.

name: Spectacle
summary: React-based presentation library from Formidable. JSX-defined slides with full React component power. Good for interactive demos. Requires React knowledge — more complex than Markdown-based alternatives.

name: Impress.js
summary: CSS3 transforms and transitions for Prezi-style spatial presentations. Novel panning/zooming between slides. Impressive demos but limited practical tooling. Mostly superseded by Reveal.js.

name: Remark (slideshow)
summary: In-browser Markdown-driven slideshow tool. Single HTML file, no build step. Simpler than Reveal.js — just Markdown in a textarea. Good for quick presentations without toolchain setup.

name: pdf2htmlEX
summary: Converts PDF to HTML while preserving text, fonts, and layout with high fidelity. Uses Poppler and FontForge. Docker image available. Best for making PDFs web-viewable without a PDF viewer.

name: Docverter
summary: HTTP API wrapper around Pandoc for document conversion. POST a file, get back the converted format. Useful as a microservice but unmaintained. Modern alternative: run Pandoc directly or use Kroki.

name: pdfTeX
summary: Default TeX engine that outputs PDF directly. Mature and stable but limited to 8-bit fonts. Included in every TeX distribution. Being gradually superseded by LuaTeX for new projects needing Unicode.

name: LuaTeX
summary: TeX engine with embedded Lua scripting and native Unicode/OpenType support. Default engine for ConTeXt MkIV. Slower than pdfTeX but far more extensible. Use when you need system fonts or Lua scripting.

name: XeTeX
summary: TeX engine with native Unicode and system font access via HarfBuzz. Produces PDF via xdvipdfmx. Popular for multilingual documents. Less scriptable than LuaTeX but faster and simpler for font switching.

name: pdf-lib
summary: Create and modify PDFs in JavaScript (browser and Node). Pure JS, no native dependencies. Supports form filling, page manipulation, and embedding. Good for programmatic PDF generation in web apps.

name: jsPDF
summary: Client-side PDF generation in JavaScript. Older than pdf-lib with a larger plugin ecosystem (html2pdf, autotable). Canvas-based rendering approach. Widely used for browser-based report generation.

name: ReportLab
summary: Python PDF generation library with low-level canvas API and high-level Platypus layout engine. Industry standard for programmatic PDF creation in Python. Commercial Plus version adds extra features.

name: FPDF2
summary: Minimalist PDF generation library for Python. Successor to PyFPDF with active maintenance. Simpler API than ReportLab — good for straightforward PDF creation without complex layouts.

name: PyMuPDF
summary: Python bindings for MuPDF. Fast PDF/EPUB text extraction, rendering, and manipulation. AGPL license — commercial license available. Best Python option for PDF reading, text extraction, and OCR integration.

name: pdfplumber
summary: PDF parsing focused on table extraction. Built on pdfminer.six. Visual debugging mode helps understand PDF structure. Best for extracting structured data from PDF reports and forms.

name: Camelot
summary: PDF table extraction using lattice (line-based) and stream (whitespace-based) methods. Returns pandas DataFrames directly. Accuracy reporting built in. Requires Ghostscript for image-based PDFs.

name: Zotero
summary: Free, open-source reference manager with browser integration, PDF annotation, and group libraries. Exports BibTeX/CSL-JSON. WebDAV sync or Zotero cloud. Best overall reference manager for researchers.

name: Mendeley
summary: Elsevier-owned reference manager. Desktop app discontinued in favor of web-only Mendeley Reference Manager. Lock-in risk increased after Elsevier acquisition. Many users migrating to Zotero.

name: BibTeX
summary: The original reference database format for LaTeX. Plain text .bib files are universally supported. Limited sorting/formatting compared to BibLaTeX but maximal compatibility with journal templates.

name: BibLaTeX
summary: Modern replacement for BibTeX with Unicode support, flexible citation styles, and the Biber backend. Superset of .bib format. Preferred for new LaTeX projects but some journals still require BibTeX.

name: Citation Style Language
summary: XML-based citation formatting language with 10,000+ styles. Used by Zotero, Mendeley, Pandoc, and most reference managers. The lingua franca of citation formatting outside LaTeX.

name: Paperpile
summary: Cloud reference manager tightly integrated with Google Docs and Google Scholar. Clean UI, BibTeX export, and browser extension. Subscription-based with no free tier. Good for Google Workspace users.

name: EndNote
summary: Long-established commercial reference manager from Clarivate. Deep Word integration and Web of Science connection. Expensive, proprietary format. Declining as researchers move to Zotero and free alternatives.

name: Vale
summary: Programmable prose linter with YAML rules. Style packages for Microsoft, Google, write-good, Joblint. Markup-aware — parses Markdown, AsciiDoc, RST natively. CI-friendly single binary.

name: textlint
summary: Pluggable linting tool for natural language. ESLint-inspired architecture with configurable rules. Strong Japanese language community. Good for enforcing style guides in documentation repos.

name: write-good
summary: Naive prose linter that flags passive voice, weasel words, and cliches. Simple but effective for catching common writing issues. Often used as a Vale style package rather than standalone.

name: LanguageTool
summary: Multilingual grammar and style checker supporting 30+ languages. Self-hostable Java server or cloud API. Open-source core with premium features. Best open alternative to Grammarly.

name: proselint
summary: Python prose linter based on advice from Strunk & White, DFW, Pinker, and other writing experts. Checks for jargon, redundancy, and mixed metaphors. Rules curated from style guides.

name: Alex
summary: Catches insensitive and inconsiderate writing. Flags gendered language, racial slurs, and profanity. Useful for inclusive documentation. Often paired with Vale or textlint in CI pipelines.

name: Read the Docs
summary: Documentation hosting platform with versioning, PR previews, and search. Builds Sphinx or MkDocs projects from git. Free for open source. The default hosting for Python library docs.

name: GitBook
summary: SaaS documentation platform with WYSIWYG editor and git sync. Pivoted from open-source CLI to closed-source SaaS. Good for non-technical contributors but significant lock-in to their platform.

name: Rustdoc
summary: Built-in Rust documentation generator. Runs doc-tests as part of cargo test. Hosted automatically on docs.rs for published crates. Tight language integration — doc comments are first-class.

name: Javadoc
summary: Standard Java API documentation generator from source code comments. Part of the JDK. Generates interlinked HTML from /** */ annotations. Established standard but dated output format.

name: MkDocs
summary: Python-based static site generator for project documentation. YAML config, Markdown content, Jinja2 themes. Material for MkDocs theme is exceptional. Simple, fast, and widely adopted.

name: Sphinx
summary: The Python documentation standard. RST-based with powerful cross-referencing, autodoc, and intersphinx. MyST adds Markdown support. More complex than MkDocs but more powerful for API documentation.

name: Docusaurus
summary: Meta-maintained docs framework with versioning, i18n, and MDX. React-powered with excellent search and sidebar navigation. Heavier than MkDocs but more customizable for complex documentation sites.

name: VitePress
summary: Vue-powered static site generator built on Vite. Successor to VuePress. Fast builds, excellent default theme, and Vue component embedding in Markdown. Growing adoption for JS ecosystem docs.

name: Starlight
summary: Astro-based documentation theme with built-in search, i18n, and accessibility. Ships minimal JS by default. Fast growing as an alternative to Docusaurus for docs that don't need heavy interactivity.

name: mdBook
summary: Rust book/docs generator. Single binary, Markdown input, GitBook-style output. Used for official Rust documentation. Fast and simple but limited customization compared to full SSGs.

name: Doxygen
summary: Documentation generator for C/C++/Java/Python from annotated source. Generates HTML, LaTeX, man pages. De facto standard for C/C++ API docs. Dated UI but unmatched for compiled language codebases.

name: Mintlify
summary: SaaS documentation platform with beautiful defaults and AI-powered search. MDX-based content, git-synced. Polished but proprietary — content is in MDX/Markdown but tooling is locked to their platform.

name: Fumadocs
summary: Next.js documentation framework with Mintlify-like aesthetics but fully open source. Full-text search, OpenAPI integration, and TypeScript-first. Rising alternative for Next.js ecosystem docs.

name: Obsidian
summary: Local-first Markdown knowledge base with bidirectional links, graph view, and plugin ecosystem. Data stored as plain .md files — no lock-in. Sync and publish available as paid services.

name: Notion
summary: All-in-one workspace with docs, databases, and wikis. Excellent collaboration but cloud-only with significant lock-in. Export is lossy. Not suitable for HPC or offline research workflows.

name: Logseq
summary: Open-source outliner and knowledge base. Stores data as Markdown or Org-mode files. Block-level linking similar to Roam. DB version in development for better performance. Privacy-focused.

name: Dendron
summary: VS Code-based hierarchical note-taking with schema enforcement. Development paused in 2023. Plain Markdown files with dot-separated hierarchy. Good concept but effectively abandoned.

name: Foam
summary: VS Code extension for Roam-like knowledge management. Wikilinks, graph visualization, daily notes. Lightweight — just a VS Code extension set over plain Markdown. Good for developers already in VS Code.

name: Roam Research
summary: Pioneered bidirectional linking and block references in note-taking. Outliner-based with Datalog queries. Expensive ($15/mo) and cloud-only. Many users migrated to Obsidian and Logseq.

name: Tana
summary: Supertag-based knowledge workspace combining outlines, databases, and AI. Highly structured data model with computed fields. Innovative but cloud-only with no export — extreme lock-in risk.

name: Anytype
summary: Local-first knowledge workspace with object-based data model and P2P sync via IPFS. Notion-like interface without the cloud dependency. Source-available (not fully open). Growing privacy-conscious user base.

name: Mermaid
summary: Text-to-diagram library supporting flowcharts, sequence diagrams, Gantt charts, class diagrams, and more. Native GitHub/GitLab rendering. De facto standard for diagrams-as-code in documentation.

name: PlantUML
summary: Text-based diagramming with extensive diagram type support (UML, C4, BPMN, network, etc.). Requires Java + Graphviz. More diagram types than Mermaid but heavier dependency chain.

name: D2
summary: Modern diagram scripting language with automatic layout. Multiple layout engines (dagre, ELK, TALA). Supports sequence diagrams, class diagrams, and custom shapes. Clean syntax, beautiful output.

name: Kroki
summary: Unified API for 20+ diagram-as-code tools. Single HTTP endpoint renders Mermaid, PlantUML, D2, Graphviz, and more. Self-hostable via Docker. Excellent for doc platforms needing multi-format diagram support.

name: Structurizr DSL
summary: DSL for the C4 model of software architecture. Define architecture once, render as PlantUML, Mermaid, D2, or interactive diagrams. Model-driven — changes propagate to all views automatically.

name: Pikchr
summary: PIC-inspired diagram markup language by the SQLite/Fossil team. Single C file, zero dependencies, outputs SVG. Embeddable anywhere. Niche but perfect for projects wanting minimal dependency diagrams.

name: Ditaa
summary: Converts ASCII art diagrams to PNG images. Write diagrams in plain text, render as graphics. Integrated into Org-mode and AsciiDoc. Charming aesthetic but limited precision compared to structured DSLs.
